name: Xata
what_it_does: Xata is a serverless database platform built on PostgreSQL with built-in full-text search (via Elasticsearch), vector search (for AI embeddings), database branching (like git branches for your schema), and a TypeScript-first REST/SDK interface. It combines structured relational data, free-text search, and vector similarity search in a single service.
use_cases: Storing agent memory and conversation history with both structured queries and full-text search in one service
Using Xata's vector store to persist and query agent-generated embeddings for semantic retrieval
Building RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines where the knowledge base lives in Xata
Branching the database schema for agent development environments without affecting production
Querying records with a combination of keyword search and structured filters in a single API call
Using the TypeScript SDK for type-safe agent data access without writing raw SQL
not_for: High-throughput transactional systems — Xata's serverless model is optimized for moderate write workloads
Teams that need complex stored procedures or advanced PostgreSQL extensions not supported by Xata
Workloads requiring direct psql/libpq access with full PostgreSQL compatibility (Xata adds abstraction layers)
Very large data sets where dedicated managed Postgres (RDS, Neon, AlloyDB) would be more appropriate
best_when: You are building an AI agent backend that needs structured data storage AND semantic/full-text search AND vector similarity search — and want all three from one service without managing Elasticsearch and a vector DB separately.
avoid_when: You need raw PostgreSQL access with extensions like PostGIS or PL/pgSQL, you have very high write throughput requirements, or your data clearly fits pure vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate) or pure relational (Neon, Supabase) without needing hybrid search.

auth.notes: Xata uses per-database API keys sent as Bearer tokens. Workspace-level keys for admin operations; database-level keys for data access. No OAuth2. Keys are generated in the Xata dashboard and should be stored as secrets. No fine-grained table-level key scoping.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier: 15GB total storage, 15 million records, 250 units of search/vector operations per month, unlimited API requests within limits
pricing.paid_tiers: Pro: $20/month (75GB storage, 250M records, more search units); Pay-as-you-go above that
pricing.notes: Free tier is generous for development and small production workloads. Vector search and full-text search consume 'units' which are tracked separately from storage. Pricing is straightforward with no per-request charges.

performance.rate_limits: Free tier: no explicit request rate limit documented, governed by compute units. Pro tier: higher compute unit allocation. Vector search operations are heavier than simple queries.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Xata returns structured JSON errors with message and status fields. Validation errors identify the specific column or constraint that failed. Query syntax errors include the invalid expression. Some errors from the underlying Elasticsearch search layer can be verbose.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Record creation with explicit IDs is idempotent. Upsert operations supported. Bulk operations support idempotency keys. Update operations are idempotent when setting the same values.
known_agent_gotchas: Xata's query language for filtering is JSON-based, not SQL — agents must use the filter object syntax
Full-text search and structured queries are separate API calls or must be combined using the search endpoint
Vector similarity search requires embedding generation before insert and query — Xata does not generate embeddings
Database branching is powerful but adds complexity — agents must specify the branch in API calls
Schema changes require migration files and cannot be made on-the-fly — plan schema upfront
Free tier search unit limits can be hit quickly with embedding-heavy workloads
No direct PostgreSQL wire protocol access — must use REST API or SDK

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced for all API connections. API keys are per-database, limiting blast radius. No row-level security built into the API layer (unlike Supabase/Postgres RLS). Data encrypted at rest. SOC2 compliant infrastructure.
